network.overall_strategy: This network models the prebiotic synthesis of threonine through 10 distinct pathways spanning three environments. Hydrothermal vents provide CO2 fixation to C1-C3 building blocks (formate, formaldehyde, pyruvate, CO) via iron-sulfur mineral catalysis. Surface environments drive two major convergent strategies: (1) the cyanosulfidic reductive homologation of HCN to glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde, followed by Strecker synthesis to the threonine aminonitrile, and (2) the prebiotic sugar pathway via aldol condensation to erythrose, then reaction with NH3 and H2S to form an activated threonine thioester. Both activated intermediates converge in the biochemical stage for hydrolysis to free threonine. Key hub molecules—formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, HCN, and 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile—create network density and ensure redundant pathways. The network is grounded in the cyanosulfidic protometabolism of Sutherland and colleagues, the prebiotic sugar pathway literature, and hydrothermal CO2 fixation studies on iron-sulfur minerals.

reactions.name: CO2 hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent, pH 9-11, H2-rich serpentinization fluids

reactions.mechanism: CO2 binds to the greigite mineral surface, which acts as an analog of biological [Fe4S4] clusters. H2 dissociatively adsorbs on the iron-sulfur surface, providing hydride equivalents. Formate is produced by insertion of CO2 into the surface metal-hydride bond, followed by release of HCOO- into solution.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate is a foundational reaction of the iron-sulfur world hypothesis. Experimental work by Roldan et al. (2015) and Preiner et al. (2020) demonstrated that Fe3S4 surfaces in alkaline hydrothermal conditions reduce CO2 to formate using H2 as reductant, mirroring the first step of the biological Wood-Ljungdahl pathway.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on native iron

conditions.other: Subseafloor hydrothermal environment, strongly reducing conditions

reactions.mechanism: Formate is further reduced on native iron surfaces (Fe0) or FeS/pyrite surfaces. The strong reducing potential of Fe0 (E0 ~ -0.44V) drives the two-electron reduction of formate to formaldehyde. The reaction is thermodynamically favorable under highly reducing hydrothermal conditions where H2 partial pressures are elevated by serpentinization.
reactions.reasoning: Reduction of formate to formaldehyde on iron surfaces has been demonstrated in simulated hydrothermal conditions. Herschy et al. (2014) showed that alkaline vent simulators generate formaldehyde from CO2 via formate intermediates using Fe0 and FeS minerals. This provides a key C1 building block for downstream sugar and amino acid synthesis.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic reductive homologation of HCN to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation (200-300nm), shallow volcanic pools, pH ~7, flow-chemistry regime

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes UV-driven reductive homologation using H2S as stoichiometric reductant. Cu(I)-Cu(II) photoredox cycling catalyzes C-C bond formation. HCN is first reduced to formaldehyde equivalents, which then undergo aldol-type coupling to form glycolaldehyde (C2). The copper coordinates with cyanide and sulfide species, stabilizing reactive intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: This is the foundational reaction of the Sutherland cyanosulfidic protometabolism. Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated that HCN reductive homologation driven by UV and H2S produces glycolaldehyde as a key C2 intermediate. Cu-mediated photoredox cycling was shown to be essential for efficient carbon-carbon bond formation from HCN feedstock. TiO2 surfaces on early Earth would have provided additional photocatalytic surfaces.

reactions.name: Formose reaction: formaldehyde self-condensation to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: Alkaline evaporitic pool with borate minerals, pH 9-11, wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of formaldehyde undergo base-catalyzed aldol condensation (the initial step of the formose reaction) to produce glycolaldehyde. Borate minerals complex with the product glycolaldehyde, stabilizing it against further uncontrolled condensation and channeling the formose reaction toward specific products.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction has long been recognized as a prebiotic source of sugars (Butlerow, 1861). Ricardo et al. (2004) demonstrated that borate minerals stabilize sugar products, particularly ribose, by forming borate-diol complexes. Montmorillonite clay surfaces concentrate formaldehyde via adsorption, facilitating the initial dimerization step. This provides an alternative to cyanosulfidic glycolaldehyde synthesis from hydrothermal formaldehyde feedstock.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: Evaporitic pool, mild alkaline pH, borate-stabilized intermediates

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde acts as the electrophilic C1 unit and glycolaldehyde provides the enolizable C2 unit. Under mildly alkaline conditions with ammonia catalysis, the aldol reaction proceeds to give glyceraldehyde (C3). Montmorillonite surfaces concentrate both reactants in the clay interlayer, facilitating the bimolecular reaction. Borate complexation protects the glyceraldehyde product.
reactions.reasoning: Aldol condensation between formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde yielding glyceraldehyde is a well-characterized step in the formose reaction network. Sutherland and colleagues showed that glyceraldehyde is a key C3 intermediate in the cyanosulfidic network, serving as a branch point toward both ribose/nucleotide and amino acid precursors (Patel et al., 2015). The prebiotic sugar pathway literature confirms that ammonia or amino acids can catalyze aldol reactions at moderate pH ~5-7 (Canavelli et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Aldol self-condensation of glycolaldehyde to erythrose

conditions.other: Concentrated evaporitic pool, pH 5-7, with ammonia organocatalysis

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of glycolaldehyde undergo aldol condensation to form tetrose sugars (erythrose and threose). One glycolaldehyde acts as the enolizable donor and the other as the electrophilic acceptor. Montmorillonite interlayer surfaces concentrate glycolaldehyde via adsorption, and ammonia catalyzes the enolization step at moderate pH (~5.2), avoiding the need for strong base.
reactions.reasoning: The aldol self-condensation of glycolaldehyde to C4 sugars including erythrose is a key step in the prebiotic sugar pathway described by Canavelli et al. (2019) and related studies. The use of ammonia or amino acid organocatalysis at pH 5.2 rather than strongly alkaline conditions makes this prebiotically plausible. Erythrose and threose then serve as precursors for threonine via the thioester pathway.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis: glyceraldehyde + HCN + NH3 to threonine aminonitrile

conditions.other: Volcanic hot spring pool, aqueous, pH 6-8

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde (as 2-hydroxypropanal tautomer/cyanohydrin) reacts with ammonia to form an imine (Schiff base). HCN then adds across the C=N bond in a stereospecific α-addition to yield 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile, the Strecker aminonitrile precursor to threonine. Montmorillonite surfaces concentrate the reactants and may provide stereochemical guidance via surface adsorption.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is one of the oldest and most robust prebiotic amino acid synthesis routes (Strecker, 1850). In the cyanosulfidic network, Sutherland and colleagues identify 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile (threonine aminonitrile, compound 16 in their nomenclature) as an 'inevitable byproduct' of the same chemistry that produces triose sugars and ribonucleotide precursors (Patel et al., 2015). The reaction proceeds through glyceraldehyde and HCN under aqueous conditions with ammonia.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis: lactaldehyde + HCN + NH3 to threonine aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde (2-hydroxypropanal) reacts with ammonia to form an imine intermediate. HCN then performs nucleophilic addition to the C=N bond to yield 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile. This is the classic Strecker mechanism applied to a C3 hydroxy-aldehyde. The chiral center at C-3 is set by the chirality of the starting lactaldehyde, while the new C-2 center is generated during the HCN addition step.
reactions.reasoning: Strecker synthesis from aldehydes, ammonia, and HCN is a canonical prebiotic amino acid route. Lactaldehyde as a substrate directly yields the threonine aminonitrile. This pathway is particularly relevant for meteoritic parent body processing, where acetaldehyde and formaldehyde react in the presence of ammonia and HCN during aqueous alteration on CM-type chondrites (Burton et al., 2012; Koga & Naraoka, 2017). Clay mineral surfaces like montmorillonite concentrate reactants and protect intermediates from hydrolysis.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: Warm prebiotic pool, mildly acidic to neutral pH, prolonged aqueous exposure

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group (-CN) of 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: first to an amide (2-amino-3-hydroxybutanamide) via nucleophilic addition of water to the nitrile, then to the free carboxylic acid (threonine) via further hydrolysis of the amide bond. The reaction is acid- or base-catalyzed and thermodynamically favorable in aqueous solution.
reactions.reasoning: Aminonitrile hydrolysis to free amino acids is the terminal step of the Strecker synthesis and is well-documented in prebiotic chemistry. The reaction occurs spontaneously in warm aqueous solution over hours to days. Patel et al. (2015) include this hydrolysis step as part of the cyanosulfidic network yielding proteinogenic amino acids. The conditions are consistent with warm prebiotic pools or the biochemical assembly environment.

reactions.name: Erythrose aminothiol rearrangement to threonine thioester

conditions.other: Volcanic hot spring, pH 5-7, one-pot aqueous system with thiol catalysis

reactions.mechanism: Erythrose undergoes beta-dehydration to form an alpha-ketoaldehyde. This ketoaldehyde reacts with NH3 and H2S to form an imine-hemithioacetal adduct. An intramolecular redox rearrangement then produces an activated threonine thioester. The sulfide mineral surface provides both catalytic sulfur species and a surface for concentrating reactants. The estimated deltaG for this type of transformation is approximately -34 kcal/mol.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction follows the prebiotic sugar pathway described by Canavelli et al. (2019) and Ritson et al. (2021), where tetrose sugars react with ammonia and H2S in a one-pot system to produce amino acid thioesters. The pathway involves dehydration, adduct formation, and redox rearrangement steps that are thermodynamically strongly favorable (deltaG ~ -34 kcal/mol). Sulfide minerals like stibnite provide catalytic sulfur and are integral to the cyanosulfidic chemistry of Sutherland's group.

reactions.name: Threonine thioester hydrolysis to free threonine

conditions.other: Prebiotic pool, aqueous environment, neutral pH. Alternatively the thioester can react with another amino acid for peptide bond formation.

reactions.mechanism: The thioester bond (C(=O)-S-R) undergoes nucleophilic attack by water, cleaving the sulfur-carbon bond and releasing free threonine (carboxylate form) and the thiol leaving group. Thioester hydrolysis is kinetically more facile than ester or amide hydrolysis, making it prebiotically relevant as an activation mechanism.
reactions.reasoning: Thioester hydrolysis to free amino acids is described in the prebiotic sugar pathway literature (Canavelli et al., 2019). The thioester intermediate is significant because it represents an activated amino acid that can either hydrolyze to the free acid or undergo aminolysis to form peptide bonds—providing a direct route from prebiotic chemistry to primitive peptide synthesis. This dual reactivity was emphasized by de Duve (1991) in the thioester world hypothesis.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to pyruvate on magnetite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent, CO2 and H2 gradients across vent walls

reactions.mechanism: CO2 is reduced stepwise on magnetite/greigite surfaces: first to formate, then to formaldehyde equivalents, then through acetaldehyde-like intermediates to pyruvate via carboxylation. The mineral surface provides both the reducing equivalents (from H2 or Fe2+) and the catalytic sites for carbon-carbon bond formation. This mirrors the reductive acetyl-CoA pathway.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate synthesis from CO2 and H2 on iron-sulfur mineral surfaces has been demonstrated by Cody et al. (2000) and Varma et al. (2018) under simulated hydrothermal conditions. Muchowska et al. (2019) showed that Fe0/FeS systems can drive a non-enzymatic analog of the reductive TCA cycle, producing pyruvate and other alpha-keto acids from CO2 and H2. This provides a direct hydrothermal route to a key C3 metabolic hub.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: Hydrothermal vent margin, moderate temperature zone

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate (an alpha-keto acid) undergoes thermal decarboxylation, losing CO2 to form acetaldehyde. The reaction is catalyzed by pyrite surfaces, where Fe2+ can coordinate with the alpha-keto group, lowering the activation energy for C-C bond cleavage. This is a well-known thermal degradation pathway of pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde is documented in hydrothermal experiments by Cody et al. (2000) and Cooper et al. (2011). The reaction occurs readily at temperatures above 100°C and is accelerated by transition metal surfaces. This provides an important link between the hydrothermal C3 metabolism and the C2 aldehyde pool needed for threonine synthesis via lactaldehyde.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of acetaldehyde and formaldehyde to lactaldehyde

conditions.other: Evaporitic pool, mild pH, concentrated by wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde is enolized under mildly basic conditions (NH3 catalysis) and attacks formaldehyde in an aldol condensation to yield 2-hydroxypropanal (lactaldehyde). Montmorillonite adsorbs and concentrates both aldehydes in the clay interlayer, promoting the bimolecular reaction. The product is a C3 hydroxy-aldehyde, the direct Strecker substrate for threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Aldol condensation between acetaldehyde and formaldehyde to yield lactaldehyde is a straightforward aldol reaction documented in prebiotic chemistry contexts. This route is particularly relevant for meteoritic parent body processing, where both acetaldehyde and formaldehyde are abundant (Meinert et al., 2016). The combination with subsequent Strecker synthesis provides a complete route from hydrothermal C2-C1 feedstocks to threonine, as discussed in analyses of Murchison meteorite amino acid formation (Burton et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch synthesis of formaldehyde from CO and H2

conditions.other: Black smoker / subseafloor hydrothermal system, gas-phase or supercritical

reactions.mechanism: CO and H2 undergo Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite surfaces to produce formaldehyde as a primary C1 oxygenated product. The reaction involves CO adsorption on the oxide surface, dissociative H2 adsorption, and stepwise hydrogenation of the surface-bound C1 species to yield formaldehyde, which desorbs from the surface.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite and other iron oxide surfaces in hydrothermal settings has been demonstrated by McCollom et al. (1999) and Holm & Andersson (2005). Formaldehyde is a primary FTT product at moderate temperatures. This provides an alternative hydrothermal route to formaldehyde independent of the formate reduction pathway, diversifying the C1 feedstock supply.

reactions.name: Reverse water-gas shift: CO2 reduction to CO on magnetite
reactions.mechanism: CO2 is reduced to CO and H2O via the reverse water-gas shift reaction on magnetite surfaces. Fe0 or Fe2+ in magnetite provides the reducing equivalents. This is thermodynamically favorable at elevated temperatures (>400K) under the high H2 partial pressures generated by serpentinization.
reactions.reasoning: The reverse water-gas shift reaction (CO2 + H2 → CO + H2O) on iron oxide surfaces at hydrothermal temperatures is well-established geochemistry (Seewald et al., 2006). CO is a critical feedstock for both Fischer-Tropsch synthesis and Acetyl-CoA-like carbon fixation. This reaction provides the bridge from CO2 fixation to CO-dependent pathways, as emphasized in the metabolism-first origin of life models (Russell & Martin, 2004).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic reductive homologation of glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation (200-300nm), shallow volcanic pool, flow-chemistry regime

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde undergoes a further reductive homologation step with HCN under UV/H2S conditions. The Cu(I)-Cu(II) photoredox cycle activates HCN for C-C bond formation with glycolaldehyde. The process involves cyanohydrin formation, followed by partial reduction of the nitrile to aldehyde, extending the carbon chain from C2 to C3 to give glyceraldehyde. This is the cyanosulfidic carbon-chain extension mechanism.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction is central to the cyanosulfidic protometabolism of Sutherland and coworkers. Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated that the UV-driven reductive homologation of HCN can sequentially build C2 (glycolaldehyde) and C3 (glyceraldehyde) aldehydes. This provides an entirely surface-photochemical route to glyceraldehyde that is independent of the formose reaction, using only HCN and H2S as feedstocks.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic HCN → glycolaldehyde → glyceraldehyde (aldol) → Strecker → threonine
pathways.description: The primary cyanosulfidic pathway where HCN is reductively homologated to glycolaldehyde via UV/H2S, then glycolaldehyde is extended to glyceraldehyde by aldol condensation with hydrothermal formaldehyde, followed by Strecker synthesis to threonine aminonitrile and hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO2 → formate → formaldehyde → formose → glyceraldehyde → Strecker → threonine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation on greigite produces formate, which is reduced to formaldehyde. Formaldehyde self-condenses (formose) to glycolaldehyde, then extends to glyceraldehyde via aldol with more formaldehyde. Strecker synthesis on glyceraldehyde yields threonine aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic glycolaldehyde → erythrose → thioester → threonine
pathways.description: Glycolaldehyde from cyanosulfidic HCN homologation undergoes aldol self-condensation to erythrose (C4). Erythrose reacts with NH3 and H2S via the prebiotic sugar pathway (dehydration, imine-hemithioacetal formation, redox rearrangement) to produce an activated threonine thioester, which hydrolyzes to free threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde → formose → erythrose → thioester → threonine
pathways.description: Formaldehyde from hydrothermal formate reduction is converted to glycolaldehyde via the formose reaction. Two glycolaldehyde molecules condense to erythrose. The prebiotic sugar pathway then converts erythrose to a threonine thioester via NH3/H2S treatment, and hydrolysis gives free threonine.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch CO → formaldehyde → formose → glyceraldehyde → Strecker → threonine
pathways.description: CO2 is first reduced to CO via reverse water-gas shift on magnetite. CO undergoes Fischer-Tropsch synthesis to formaldehyde. Formaldehyde self-condenses to glycolaldehyde, then further condenses with formaldehyde to glyceraldehyde. Strecker synthesis yields threonine aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch CO → formaldehyde → formose → erythrose → thioester → threonine
pathways.description: Same initial hydrothermal pathway as P5 (CO2 → CO → formaldehyde → glycolaldehyde), but then glycolaldehyde self-condenses to erythrose instead of glyceraldehyde. Erythrose follows the prebiotic sugar pathway to threonine thioester and then hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate → acetaldehyde → lactaldehyde → Strecker → threonine
pathways.description: CO2 is reduced directly to pyruvate on magnetite/greigite. Pyruvate thermally decarboxylates on pyrite to acetaldehyde. Acetaldehyde condenses with formaldehyde (from hydrothermal sources) to yield lactaldehyde. Strecker synthesis on lactaldehyde with HCN/NH3 produces threonine aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Fully cyanosulfidic: HCN → glycolaldehyde → glyceraldehyde (homologation) → Strecker → threonine
pathways.description: An entirely surface-photochemical pathway where HCN is reductively homologated to glycolaldehyde (rxn_003), then glycolaldehyde is further homologated to glyceraldehyde via additional HCN/H2S/UV chemistry (rxn_017), without requiring hydrothermal formaldehyde. Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis complete the route.

pathways.name: Mixed hydrothermal-cyanosulfidic: hydrothermal HCHO + cyanosulfidic glycolaldehyde → glyceraldehyde → Strecker → threonine
pathways.description: Cross-environment convergence pathway where formaldehyde from hydrothermal CO2 fixation (via formate) combines with glycolaldehyde from cyanosulfidic HCN homologation in a surface evaporitic pool. Aldol condensation yields glyceraldehyde, which then undergoes Strecker synthesis to threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate → acetaldehyde + Fischer-Tropsch formaldehyde → lactaldehyde → Strecker → threonine
pathways.description: Dual hydrothermal origin pathway where both C2 (acetaldehyde, from pyruvate decarboxylation) and C1 (formaldehyde, from Fischer-Tropsch on CO) are produced hydrothermally. These combine in surface aldol condensation to lactaldehyde, followed by Strecker synthesis to threonine aminonitrile and hydrolysis.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Solid cyanosulfidic pathway. The aldol step (rxn_005) requires formaldehyde that is not produced within this pathway itself; it must be supplied from a hydrothermal sub-pathway (P2 or P5), making P1 not fully self-contained. However, as part of the network this is fine. The Strecker synthesis on glyceraldehyde is directly supported by Sutherland's work (threonine aminonitrile as 'inevitable byproduct').
suggestions: Specify the source of formaldehyde more explicitly within pathway description
Note UV requirement for cyanosulfidic chemistry at surface

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Complete CO2-to-threonine pathway with excellent environmental flow (Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical). The weakest link is rxn_002 (formate reduction to formaldehyde on native iron), which is thermodynamically uphill and has limited experimental demonstration at hydrothermal temperatures where formaldehyde is thermally unstable. The formose reaction (rxn_004) needs mineral base catalyst (e.g., Ca(OH)2 or borate) which should be specified. Overall a strong and complete pathway.
suggestions: Specify mineral base or borate catalyst for formose reaction
Address formaldehyde thermal stability concerns in hydrothermal regime
Consider adding a surface UV-driven reduction of formate as alternative to rxn_002

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Clean implementation of the Prebiotic Sugar Pathway described in literature. The erythrose aminothiol rearrangement (rxn_010) is well-supported by literature describing β-dehydration, imine-hemithioacetal adduct formation, and intramolecular redox rearrangement. The thioester product is biologically significant as it is pre-disposed to peptide bond formation. Minor concern: the aldol self-condensation of glycolaldehyde (rxn_006) may also produce threose and erythrulose as competing products, reducing yield to erythrose specifically.
suggestions: Acknowledge competing tetrose isomers from rxn_006
Note that the thioester intermediate also enables peptide formation, adding biological relevance

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Complete hydrothermal-to-biochemical pathway merging the formose and prebiotic sugar pathway strategies. Same rxn_002 weakness as P2. Good three-environment progression. The convergence of two well-documented prebiotic chemical systems (formose + aminothiol rearrangement) strengthens this pathway's plausibility.
suggestions: Consider borate stabilization of glycolaldehyde to improve selectivity in formose chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Alternative CO fixation route via reverse water-gas shift. The RWGS (rxn_016) on magnetite is experimentally validated. Fischer-Tropsch formaldehyde synthesis (rxn_015) is plausible on iron-nickel catalysts at hydrothermal conditions but typically yields a range of products with formaldehyde as a minor component. Downstream chemistry is solid.
suggestions: Specify iron-nickel alloy or awaruite as catalyst for rxn_015
Note low selectivity for formaldehyde in FT synthesis

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Parallel of P5 but through erythrose/thioester route instead of Strecker. Provides useful redundancy. Same Fischer-Tropsch selectivity concern.

Novel pyruvate-based route. The direct reduction of CO2 to pyruvate on magnetite (rxn_012) has some experimental support from Cody et al. and more recent studies, but yields are very low and the reaction is debated. Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde (rxn_013) is well-established. The lactaldehyde intermediate is a creative alternative substrate for Strecker synthesis. Pathway requires formaldehyde from another sub-pathway for rxn_014.
suggestions: Cite specific experimental evidence for hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis
Consider FeS/NiS mixed catalysts for rxn_012
Note that pathway requires HCHO from hydrothermal sub-network

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The most literature-aligned pathway, directly implementing the Sutherland cyanosulfidic protometabolism. Both homologation steps (HCN→glycolaldehyde and glycolaldehyde→glyceraldehyde) are supported by experimental work from the Sutherland group. The threonine aminonitrile is described in literature as an 'inevitable byproduct' of exactly this chemistry. Environmental progression is Surface→Biochemical (no hydrothermal step), which is acceptable for the cyanosulfidic model that relies on UV surface photochemistry. Missing explicit mention of Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling and UV irradiation.
suggestions: Add Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling as explicit catalyst for rxn_003 and rxn_017
Specify UV irradiation conditions for surface cyanosulfidic reactions
This is the strongest pathway - consider featuring it more prominently

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good demonstration of cross-environment convergence: hydrothermal formaldehyde meeting cyanosulfidic glycolaldehyde at the surface for aldol condensation. This is exactly the kind of convergent network topology that makes the overall network robust. The mixing of two independent feedstock streams at the surface is geochemically plausible (vent effluent mixing with surface pool chemistry).
suggestions: Elaborate on the geochemical scenario for mixing of hydrothermal and surface chemistries

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Most complex pathway, drawing on two independent hydrothermal channels (pyruvate and Fischer-Tropsch) converging at the surface via aldol condensation. Demonstrates maximum network integration. Accumulates the uncertainties of both rxn_012 and rxn_015, but the redundancy provided by the network means that if either upstream source is weak, alternative pathways compensate.
suggestions: Acknowledge accumulated uncertainty from two less-established hydrothermal reactions

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an excellent and well-designed synthesis network for L-threonine that demonstrates strong alignment with the prebiotic chemistry literature. The network successfully implements the two major literature-supported strategies: (1) the Sutherland cyanosulfidic protometabolism with reductive homologation of HCN to glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde followed by Strecker synthesis, and (2) the prebiotic sugar pathway via aldol condensation to erythrose and aminothiol rearrangement to activated thioesters. The network topology is strong with 18 molecules, 17 reactions, and 10 pathways showing genuine convergence through 4 well-identified hub molecules. Formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde serve as excellent hub molecules connecting hydrothermal and surface chemistries. The environmental progression (Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical) is well-maintained across all pathways, with hydrothermal reactions properly specifying mineral catalysts (greigite, magnetite, native iron). Key strengths: (1) Direct alignment with Sutherland's cyanosulfidic work, including the threonine aminonitrile as 'inevitable byproduct'; (2) Implementation of the prebiotic sugar pathway with activated thioester intermediates; (3) Multiple redundant pathways providing robustness; (4) Proper hub molecule identification creating genuine network density. Areas for improvement: (1) Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling and UV irradiation should be explicitly specified for cyanosulfidic reactions; (2) Surface reactions should specify mineral catalysts (montmorillonite for aldol, borate for sugar stabilization); (3) The stereochemical challenge (four possible diastereomers) is not addressed but is acknowledged as an open problem in the field; (4) rxn_002 (formate→formaldehyde) and rxn_012 (CO2→pyruvate) have limited experimental support and are the weakest links; (5) The concentration problem noted in literature could be addressed by specifying clay adsorption or evaporative concentration mechanisms. Overall, this network represents a high-quality, literature-grounded model of prebiotic threonine synthesis that correctly prioritizes the most experimentally supported pathways while providing alternative routes for robustness.
